Competitive erosion from commoditization is much more likely because the AI capability itself is already commoditized, and there's no defensible differentiation.
options.explanation: This sounds strategically reasonable but contradicts the scenario setup. If a law firm could get the same legal drafting from free GPT-4, they would already be using it—they're paying $8k/month, which means the current product is doing something GPT-4 alone doesn't do (likely: fine-tuned on legal cases, integrated into their workflow, or trained on their firm's precedent). The answer assumes the product has no differentiation today, but the paying customers prove otherwise. This is a plausible-but-wrong reasoning trap: it sounds like a competitive threat, but it actually assumes away the problem Carlos is trying to solve. It optimizes for 'defending against commoditization' rather than 'understanding why customers actually stay.'

list.step_nudge: What are the ways to build a real moat in AI, and which one does Carlos actually own?

questions.question_text: Could Carlos own legal data, legal expertise, lawyer relationships, or model quality — and which of those is actually scarce?

options.option_text: Legal data and lawyer relationships are scarce. Data is defensible because it accumulates over time and competitors can't retroactively collect it. Relationships are scarce because trust compounds—three paying customers create a moat only if they become advocates who introduce you to other firms. Model quality alone is not scarce; it commoditizes in 6–12 months as base models improve. Legal expertise is everywhere (law schools produce thousands of lawyers). The real scarcity is the combination: proprietary case outcomes + relationship capital from happy customers.
options.explanation: This answer identifies the two scarce, defensible assets (data accumulation + relationship network) while correctly dismissing the false moats (model quality, expertise supply). It shows the reasoning move: distinguish what's actually scarce from what merely feels defensible. It also hints at the second-order effect—happy customers become distribution.

options.option_text: Model quality and proprietary legal data are both scarce if the startup owns a unique training dataset that competitors can't easily replicate. Fine-tuning on proprietary case law and outcomes creates a moat. Lawyer relationships matter but are secondary—they're a result of having a good product, not the source of defensibility.
options.explanation: This answer correctly identifies data as scarce and defensible. It grasps that accumulated datasets are hard to replicate. However, it undervalues relationships and overvalues model quality as a sustainable moat. It misses the insight that model quality is intrinsically time-limited (6 months per the scenario), whereas relationships compound over time.

options.option_text: All four are scarce: legal data, expertise, relationships, and model quality. The startup should pursue all of them because they collectively create a defensible position. The key is to not choose—build data, hire experts, nurture relationships, and improve the model in parallel.
options.explanation: This avoids the core reasoning move of the frame: scarcity is relative and comparative. Not everything is equally scarce or equally defensible. With 8 weeks of cash, a six-person team cannot pursue all four. This answer treats the question as a checklist rather than a strategic ranking, which is the junior mistake.

options.option_text: Model quality is the only truly scarce asset because fine-tuning on proprietary data is what competitors will copy fastest. The three customers will eventually demand feature parity with competitors anyway, so building relationships now is a waste of time. Carlos should focus entirely on making his model so good that no competitor can catch up in 18 months, then raise Series A on pure technical defensibility.
options.explanation: This sounds strategic but reverses the scarcity logic. Model quality *commoditizes fastest*, not slowest. Competitors with more capital can out-fine-tune Carlos in 6–9 months. The answer also discounts customer relationships, which are actually the most defensible because they're relationship-specific and sticky. It optimizes for the wrong scarcity.

questions.question_text: What if the moat is not the model, but the understanding of lawyer workflow that Carlos has from his three customers?

options.option_text: The moat is Carlos's ability to iterate with real lawyers and discover what workflows they actually need—not just what they say they need. This understanding compounds: each feature his team ships teaches them more about where the real friction points are, making it harder for competitors to catch up because they don't have that lived context. The model itself becomes a commodity; the insight into what lawyers will pay for is the defensible advantage.
options.explanation: This answer recognizes that the moat question has shifted from technical (the model) to organizational (the knowledge). It identifies the second-order effect: each customer interaction teaches Carlos something a well-funded competitor won't naturally learn without their own customer base. It reframes the trade-off—shipping features IS building the moat, not the alternative to it. This is the core reasoning move for 'list': expanding the stakeholder map to include the lawyers' unstated needs, and recognizing that understanding lawyer workflow is a structural advantage.

options.option_text: Carlos should focus on shipping features because customers reveal what actually matters to them in practice. By talking to lawyers while building, he'll uncover problems the model alone can't solve, and that will give him an edge over competitors who just fine-tune generic LLMs. The data from real usage will eventually improve the model anyway.
options.explanation: This answer correctly identifies that customer work teaches you things and that lawyers reveal real needs. It gestures toward the moat-through-understanding idea. However, it frames the advantage as something that 'eventually' helps the model, rather than naming the moat itself as the workflow insight. It doesn't fully articulate why lawyer understanding is structurally harder to copy. It's in the right direction but stops short of the reasoning move: that the moat is not a technical artifact but an organizational knowledge base.

options.option_text: Carlos should ship features because customers are asking for them, which means there's proven demand. Competitors can copy the model, but Carlos has proof three law firms will pay for this product, and that's more valuable right now than a better-tuned AI that no one uses.
options.explanation: This answer correctly identifies the immediate tactical advantage—proof of product-market fit—but it only addresses the symptom: customers want features. It doesn't engage with the deeper question about what makes the business defensible long-term. It treats 'building moat' and 'shipping features' as separate paths, missing the possibility that shipping features IS how you build a moat. This is a junior answer that solves the visible urgency without thinking about the underlying structure.

options.option_text: The real moat is the lawyer workflow understanding, so Carlos should pause feature work entirely and spend the next two months building a comprehensive map of every lawyer process—discovery, motion drafting, precedent research—so he has documentation competitors can't replicate. Then ship features based on that map.
options.explanation: This sounds strategic but it inverts the reasoning. Moats based on workflow understanding are built *through* iterative engagement with customers, not through abstraction and documentation beforehand. Pausing customer work to document workflows is a false certainty—lawyers won't reveal their real workflows until Carlos is actually helping them solve problems. This optimizes for the wrong thing: it treats workflow understanding as something you can capture upfront and lock in, rather than something you continuously learn from real usage. It also wastes the eight-week runway on non-revenue work, contradicting the original constraint.

questions.question_text: Are there ways to serve customers and improve the moat simultaneously, or are they genuinely in conflict?

options.option_text: Yes. Customer work on specific use cases (e.g., contract analysis, precedent discovery) forces the model to handle real legal complexity that generic fine-tuning misses. Each customer feature request reveals data gaps and edge cases that make the model more robust. The moat isn't just fine-tuning—it's the feedback loop between customers and the model. Two months of feature shipping with careful data collection could generate more valuable training signals than two months of isolated fine-tuning.
options.explanation: This answer recognizes the false dichotomy. It shows that serving customers *is* a form of moat-building—the key insight is that customer-driven feature work generates proprietary data and real-world complexity that competitors copying the approach won't have. It reframes the trade-off by identifying the hidden synergy: customer feedback improves the model's actual defensibility.

options.option_text: Partially. Building features that customers need will naturally improve the model on those specific use cases, but this doesn't necessarily create a lasting moat if competitors can achieve similar results with their own customer base. The moat requires systematic data accumulation and model depth that pure feature shipping doesn't guarantee. Carlos needs both paths but should sequence them strategically.
options.explanation: This answer acknowledges the potential overlap but stops short of identifying *how* customer work builds moat specifically. It recognizes that sequencing matters and that neither path alone is sufficient, but it doesn't articulate the mechanism by which customer data becomes defensible advantage. It's partially right but lacks the insight that proprietary use-case knowledge is itself a moat.

options.option_text: No, they are in direct conflict. Fine-tuning requires deep work on the model itself, while building features requires shipping quickly. The team can't do both with only six people and eight weeks of cash. Carlos must choose: either become technically superior or become revenue-focused. Trying both will result in doing neither well.
options.explanation: This answer identifies only the surface constraint—time and headcount—without examining whether the underlying work actually conflicts. It treats moat and revenue as independent activities rather than exploring whether they could be coupled. It's a risk-averse answer that doesn't test the premise of the question.

options.option_text: Yes, but only if Carlos deprioritizes fine-tuning entirely and focuses on shipping features. The customer requests themselves will naturally improve the model through inference-time scaling and real-world usage. He doesn't need proprietary fine-tuning if he scales fast enough to outrun competitors. Revenue solves the moat problem by funding better infrastructure later.
options.explanation: This sounds strategic but conflates two different things: inference-time improvement (which is real) with moat-building (which requires systematic data advantage). It assumes scaling and speed alone create defensibility, which contradicts the scenario's core tension—competitors can ship basic LLM layers quickly too. It's optimizing for growth when the real threat is commoditization, and it underestimates the six-month competitive window Carlos faces.

optimize.step_nudge: Carlos is optimizing for defensibility, but at the cost of capital efficiency and customer proof.

questions.question_text: If Carlos ships customer features now and raises on thirty k MRR, how much runway does that buy him to build the moat later?

options.option_text: Raising at $30k MRR gives Carlos roughly 12–18 months of runway, but only if he reaches that MRR milestone within the next 6–8 weeks. He needs to model: (a) how much capital he'll raise at that valuation, (b) monthly burn post-raise, and (c) how many months that runway actually buys before he must ship the moat or risk running out of cash again. The real constraint isn't time—it's whether $30k MRR proves the business enough to raise at a valuation high enough to fund moat-building without another crunch.
options.explanation: This answer demonstrates the core optimize move: naming the actual optimization criterion (runway as a function of valuation, not just months), articulating the trade-off (growth rate vs. capital efficiency), and identifying the hidden constraint (raising enough capital at a defensible valuation). It shows why 'shipping features now' is only a win if it actually unlocks sufficient capital—otherwise the timeline pressure just returns in 6 months.

options.option_text: Raising on $30k MRR should give him several months to a year of extra runway depending on investor appetite and burn rate. The key is that revenue makes the startup less risky, so he'll likely raise at a better valuation than he would today, which directly extends how long he can survive while building the moat.
options.explanation: This answer grasps the right direction—that revenue improves fundraising outcomes and extends runway—but stops short of the full reasoning. It doesn't name the specific metric (runway calculation), doesn't quantify or model the cash impact, and doesn't identify the second-order risk: that even with 'extra runway,' if the moat timeline stretches or competitive pressure accelerates, he still faces a crunch. It's intuitive but lacks the precision needed for actual decision-making.

options.option_text: If Carlos hits $30k MRR, he'll have proven the product works and proven the market wants it. That proof is valuable to investors, so he should be able to raise enough money to buy him time. Exactly how much depends on who he pitches to, but more runway is definitely better than staying at $24k MRR.
options.explanation: This answer treats the symptom (traction = easier fundraising) without modeling the actual constraint. It acknowledges that proof matters but doesn't quantify the runway or ask the hard questions: How much capital is 'enough'? At what valuation? Given what burn rate? It's the kind of reasoning that feels reassuring in a pitch meeting but doesn't survive a spreadsheet.

options.option_text: Shipping features now and raising at $30k MRR buys him roughly 18–24 months of runway because investors will fund a proven, revenue-generating business generously. That's plenty of time to build the moat without the time pressure that currently exists. The risk of competitors is now solved by money.
options.explanation: This sounds reassuring but optimizes for the wrong thing. It assumes that capital + time automatically solve the moat problem, but ignores the second-order risk: competitors have the same capital, and they're building their own models in parallel. Runway alone doesn't guarantee Carlos can outpace better-funded rivals in model-building. The answer mistakes 'having time' for 'having advantage.' It also misses that 18–24 months of runway is only achievable at a specific valuation multiple—VCs won't fund a $100k ARR company at a $20M valuation just because it has traction.

questions.question_text: What happens to his competitive advantage if he waits two months and a funded competitor launches in the meantime?

options.option_text: [Option A — needs content]
options.explanation: Demonstrates the correct reasoning move for this step.

options.option_text: [Option B — needs content]
options.explanation: Right direction but incomplete.

options.option_text: [Option C — needs content]
options.explanation: Addresses surface symptom only.

options.option_text: [Option D — needs content]
options.explanation: Sounds reasonable but is wrong.

questions.question_text: Is the bottleneck his ability to serve customers, or his ability to train the model faster than competitors?

options.option_text: The bottleneck is his ability to train the model faster than competitors. If competitors can replicate his approach in six months with just compute and data, then model training speed is the clock ticking down. Customer feature requests are secondary—they're asking for polish on a commodity product if the moat disappears. The real question isn't whether customers want features; it's whether Carlos can defensibly own the core capability before money runs out.
options.explanation: This answer correctly identifies the critical constraint: time-to-moat versus time-to-commoditization. It applies the optimize step's core move—naming the actual bottleneck, not the loudest request. The reasoning acknowledges that customer revenue without defensibility is a false signal of product-market fit. It distinguishes between what customers say they need (features) and what Carlos actually needs to survive (defensible IP). This is the kind of second-order thinking that separates strategy from triage.

options.option_text: The bottleneck is model training speed, but only if his three customers would stay long enough for him to build the moat. If competitive pressure will force those customers to leave within six months anyway, then the timeline for training might be irrelevant. The real bottleneck could be customer retention—if he can't keep revenue while training, the moat work has no revenue to defend.
options.explanation: This answer shows good intuition about constraints being interdependent—it recognizes that a moat is only valuable if there's still a business to defend. However, it conflates two separate questions: (1) Can he train a moat? (2) Can he keep customers while training? The optimize step requires naming which constraint is binding first. This answer hedges by saying the bottleneck depends on customer stickiness, but doesn't commit to which is actually the blocking constraint. It's directionally right but lacks the crisp, testable framing that strategy requires.

options.option_text: The bottleneck is whether customers will pay for the features they're asking for. If Carlos builds the features and customers don't adopt them, he'll have spent his limited time on the wrong thing. The risk is wasted effort on feature development that doesn't drive retention or upgrade value.
options.explanation: This answer addresses the visible tactical concern—feature risk—but misses the strategic frame. It treats the bottleneck as 'will this feature drive revenue' rather than 'what will actually determine whether this business survives.' In a six-month commoditization window, feature adoption is downstream of whether the core product remains defensible. This answer optimizes locally (maximize feature ROI) without considering the broader constraint (time to moat). It's the kind of thinking that leads to polishing a leaking boat.

options.option_text: The bottleneck is actually neither—it's having enough cash. Both moat-building and customer feature work require time and money. The real answer is that Carlos should raise money immediately to buy himself time to do both. With more runway, he can afford to work on the moat while keeping customers happy with incremental features.
options.explanation: This sounds pragmatic but actually dodges the strategic decision. Raising money might be part of the answer, but it doesn't resolve the core trade-off—it just delays the choice. If Carlos can't raise without traction (which is typical for early-stage companies), he's back to the original constraint. More importantly, this answer treats the problem as a resource shortage rather than a prioritization problem. The optimize step requires naming what's truly binding (time-to-moat vs. time-to-commoditization), not just adding more resources. It's the tempting but lazy way out of hard choices.

win.step_nudge: Carlos should ship customer features now and use the revenue to fund moat-building at scale.

questions.question_text: What specific customer request should Carlos prioritize first to prove that the business model works?

options.option_text: Prioritize the feature request that would cause a customer to expand their seat count or contract value without requiring a new customer acquisition—something that proves the unit economics work and makes the business defensible by showing revenue growth without the moat.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the core win reasoning: Carlos needs to prove the business model works—not just that customers like the AI, but that they'll pay more as they get more value. A feature that drives expansion revenue proves product-market fit and justifies raising capital, which buys time for the moat-building work. It also implicitly reframes the false choice: both paths are necessary, but revenue-first proves the model is worth defending before investing in the moat.

options.option_text: Prioritize whichever customer request solves the most painful bottleneck in their workflow, because removing friction will generate word-of-mouth referrals and reduce churn before competitors enter the market.
options.explanation: This correctly identifies that customer pain is the lever—high-impact features do drive retention and referrals, both crucial under time pressure. But it optimizes for growth/retention without explicitly testing whether customers will pay more for the solution. Word-of-mouth and churn prevention help, but don't prove the business model works in the way Carlos needs: they don't generate revenue proof for a fundraise.

options.option_text: Focus on the feature request from the largest paying customer first, since they represent the most revenue at risk if a competitor copies the core capability.
options.explanation: This addresses the obvious symptom—protect your biggest customer—but misses the strategic insight. Protecting one customer by building features doesn't prove the business model works at scale or to investors. The real test is whether customers will expand spend or whether the product can attract new customers. Prioritizing by revenue size alone is reactive, not diagnostic of business viability.

options.option_text: Hold off on feature requests entirely and use the eight weeks to ship the fine-tuned model as an MVP upgrade, charging a premium tier for access and using early revenue to fund both the moat and customer features.
options.explanation: This sounds strategically sophisticated—monetizing the moat work—but actually violates the constraint Carlos faces: he has eight weeks of cash and no capital. Building the fine-tuned model takes two months by his own estimate, leaving six weeks to land customers on a new product. This increases risk on both axes: it delays customer revenue proof and delays the moat work. It also assumes customers will pay premium pricing before he's proven they even want the upgrade.

questions.question_text: How should he measure whether the two-month bet on customer features was the right call?

options.option_text: In eight weeks, measure whether the three existing customers expanded their usage (more cases processed, higher monthly spend, or expansion into new practice areas). If they didn't expand despite new features, the features didn't solve a real problem—meaning the revenue path was a false signal. If they did expand AND new inbound deal conversations started, he has proof that customer problems are real and repeatable, which justifies raising on traction. The test: existing customer expansion rate + new inbound pipeline velocity both above zero.
options.explanation: This nails the win-step reasoning: a falsifiable test that distinguishes between 'customers are paying because we exist' vs. 'customers are paying because we solved a real repeatable problem.' Expansion of existing customers is the truest signal that the features created defensible value. New inbound validates market pull, not just founder persuasion. Both together prove the revenue path was the right bet—and give him a real funding narrative. The threshold (both above zero in 8 weeks) is aggressive but defensible for a capital-constrained startup.

options.option_text: Measure monthly recurring revenue (MRR) growth from $24k to $30k+ and track the number of feature requests from those three customers versus inbound requests from non-customers. If MRR stayed flat and all requests came from existing customers, the features were defensive (keeping churn at bay) but didn't create new value. If MRR grew and he started getting inbound, the customer path was right.
options.explanation: This correctly identifies that MRR growth alone is insufficient—he needs to distinguish between 'customers paid us anyway' and 'customers paid us more because we solved something.' The distinction between existing-customer requests and inbound is good. However, it misses the critical insight: he should measure whether *existing customers expanded*, not just whether inbound arrived. Inbound without expansion of existing customers could mean he picked the wrong features, or it could mean the market wants the problem solved but not by him. Expansion is the tightest proof.

options.option_text: Track whether the three customers renew their contracts at the end of the eight-week period. If they all renew, the features were valuable and the customer path was correct. If any churn, the moat path should have been chosen.
options.explanation: This addresses the visible symptom (did customers stay?) but not the underlying question. Renewal only tells you whether customers felt the product was worth keeping—not whether the *feature bet* was right. Customers might renew out of switching costs, inertia, or because other parts of the product were valuable. This confuses 'customer retention' with 'feature validation.' It also ignores the second half of the win-step test: whether he has proof the business works *at scale*. Renewal ≠ proof that the revenue path creates a defensible business.

options.option_text: Measure the total amount of development time spent on moat-building activities during those eight weeks versus time spent on customer features. If less than 25% of engineering effort went to moat work, the customer path was correct because he maximized revenue velocity. If more than 25% went to moat, he should have committed fully to the moat instead of splitting focus.
options.explanation: This sounds strategic (measure your commitment level) but actually optimizes for the wrong thing. The question is not 'did we prioritize correctly given our constraints?' but 'was the bet—features over moat—the right call?' Effort allocation is a tactical output, not a test of the underlying hypothesis. You could spend 5% of time on features and still make the wrong bet if those features didn't drive expansion or inbound. Conversely, you could spend 80% on features and make the right bet if the features created real defensible value. This confuses process with outcomes.

questions.question_text: If competitors launch a comparable product within six months, what will actually protect Carlos' customers from switching?

options.option_text: Switching costs embedded in their workflows: deep integrations with their existing case management systems, custom legal templates built on top of Carlos' model, and trained internal processes that make the AI output actionable. A competitor's generic product requires re-integration and retraining.
options.explanation: This identifies the actual moat in a six-month window: not model superiority (which competitors can match), but operational lock-in. It shows that switching costs come from integration depth and workflow embeddedness, not from the AI itself. This is the defensible position without waiting for proprietary fine-tuning to compound. It's specific to the legal domain and recognizes that customers choose based on friction, not just capability parity.

options.option_text: Relationship and domain trust: the three customers have built confidence in Carlos' team's understanding of their specific legal problems, and they'll stick with a known partner over an untested alternative even if the competitor's model is comparable.
options.explanation: This correctly identifies that trust and relationship matter in B2B legal services, and that switching isn't purely rational. However, it doesn't address the mechanism that *protects* them—relationships alone aren't a moat if the competitor offers demonstrably better outcomes or lower cost. It's missing the structural element (workflow lock-in, data lock-in, or integration depth) that makes switching actually costly or painful.

options.option_text: Proven accuracy: if Carlos' model consistently delivers better legal analysis and argument quality than competitors, customers will recognize superior value and stay loyal.
options.explanation: This addresses only the visible symptom—quality of the core product. But the scenario explicitly states that competitors can copy the approach within six months, which means model quality is not a lasting moat. This answer confuses 'why customers like the product today' with 'why they won't switch tomorrow when someone copies it.' It's the trap Carlos is trying to escape.

options.option_text: Proprietary legal datasets: Carlos should ensure his customers' case data and legal documents stay locked into his platform so competitors can't access the same training material, making it impossible for them to build an equivalent model.
options.explanation: This sounds protective but actually contradicts good business practice and may create legal liability. It frames the moat as controlling *customer data* rather than creating genuine switching costs or value stickiness. It's also ethically questionable in a professional services context and would likely damage trust. The real protection comes from what you *do with* the data (integration and workflow embeddedness), not from hostaging it.

title: The Silent Bug and the Bigger Drop
scenario_context: Priya is a developer at Lens, a consumer photo-sharing app. The PM flagged a bug where the 'share to story' button sometimes fails silently, causing users to abandon the action. Priya quickly identified and fixed a race condition in the upload queue that's been present for three weeks.
scenario_trigger: Upon investigation, Priya discovers that story shares have actually been declining for seven weeks—long before the bug appeared three weeks ago. This means the bug fix alone won't solve the core problem.
scenario_question: Should Priya ship the bug fix now and close the ticket, or should she first investigate what caused the original 40% drop in story shares seven weeks ago?
engineer_standout: Look at the dates. The drop started before the bug existed. What does that tell you about cause and effect?

frame.step_nudge: Define what problem you're actually solving. Fixing the reported bug and solving the real problem are not the same thing.

questions.question_text: The bug is real and you fixed it. But look at the timeline: what does the gap between the drop (week 1) and the bug (week 3) tell you about what's actually happening?

options.option_text: The bug didn't cause the drop—it was created by it. A two-week gap between symptom and root cause means something changed user behavior first (maybe a feature update, a competing app, or a UI change), then users started encountering the broken button as a secondary issue. The bug didn't make sharing less appealing; whatever happened seven weeks ago did. Fixing the bug alone won't recover the lost users.
options.explanation: This answer correctly identifies the causal sequence: the drop is the symptom, the bug is a symptom of the symptom. It understands that the seven-week timeline is not random—something changed in week 1 that made users less likely to share, and then three weeks later they discovered the button was broken. It avoids the trap of assuming fixing the bug solves the problem. It shows root-cause vs. symptom distinction.

options.option_text: The gap suggests there were multiple failures. The bug is real, but the bigger drop implies users were already sharing less before they hit the silent failure. There may be another issue—maybe performance, discovery, or user interest—that we haven't found yet. The bug fix is necessary but probably not sufficient.
options.explanation: This answer gets the right direction: it recognizes that the bug alone doesn't explain the drop, and that multiple factors are at play. However, it stops at 'there may be another issue' without pushing the reasoning further. It doesn't articulate the causal sequence clearly—that the drop happened *first*, making it the primary problem, and the bug became visible only afterward. It's on the right track but lacks the specificity and conviction of a frame-level insight.

options.option_text: The bug has been causing silent failures for three weeks, and it's finally showing up in the data. The 40% drop is the cumulative effect of users hitting the broken button and giving up. We should ship the fix right away and then monitor to see if shares recover.
options.explanation: This answer mistakes the timeline: it assumes the bug caused the drop, when the drop actually *preceded* the bug. It focuses on the visible problem (the broken button) without investigating the gap. It's a reactive answer that optimizes for closing the ticket rather than understanding what actually happened. It would lead to shipping a fix that solves a secondary problem while the primary driver of user loss remains unknown.

options.option_text: The gap between the drop and the bug suggests they're unrelated—the drop must be due to seasonal factors, market saturation, or user churn that's inevitable. The bug is a separate issue that happens to coincide with the drop. Fix the bug, but don't expect it to move the needle on the bigger trend.
options.explanation: This answer sounds reasonable on the surface (acknowledging the gap) but actually detaches the two events completely. It misses the key insight: the gap is *evidence* of a causal chain, not evidence of independence. By treating the bug and the drop as coincidental, it abandons the investigation at exactly the moment when the timeline is most revealing. It's a reasoning trap dressed up as pragmatism.

questions.question_text: Priya could fix the button and declare victory. What assumption would she be making about why users stopped sharing stories?

options.option_text: That the technical bug is the root cause of the 40% drop—when in fact the bug only accelerated a decline that started for a different reason, and fixing the button alone won't restore lost sharing behavior.
options.explanation: This option identifies the core frame move: distinguishing symptom from root cause. The bug is a *symptom* of users not wanting to share, not the *cause*. The 7-week/3-week timeline gap is the critical data point—fixing the button treats the visible failure mode but ignores why sharing appeal dropped in the first place. This is the right mental model for the frame step: why-before-how.

options.option_text: That the drop is purely technical in nature and can be solved with code—without investigating what product or user-experience changes happened seven weeks ago that made sharing less appealing.
options.explanation: This captures the right insight that there's a missing investigation, but frames it too narrowly as 'technical vs. product.' It hints at the real problem (something changed seven weeks ago) but doesn't articulate the key assumption: that Priya is conflating a *failure mode* with a *root cause*. It's directionally correct but lacks the precision of option A.

options.option_text: That users will start sharing again as soon as the button works reliably, because the feature itself is appealing and only the execution was broken.
options.explanation: This stays at the symptom level—it assumes the feature is fine and just needs the bug fixed. It acknowledges the button is broken but misses the core insight: there's a separate, older reason why users stopped *wanting* to share in the first place. This is what a junior engineer might conclude after fixing the bug.

options.option_text: That the 40% drop is actually due to the race condition getting progressively worse over time, and shipping her fix will restore sharing to pre-seven-week levels.
options.explanation: This sounds logical on the surface—bugs do get progressively worse—but it's contradicted by the data. The bug only existed for three weeks, but the drop started seven weeks ago. This assumes the bug *explains* the entire decline, which the timeline disproves. It's a plausible-sounding error that would lead Priya to ship and miss the real problem.

questions.question_text: If you ship the fix without understanding the seven-week drop, what risk are you taking on?

options.option_text: You're treating a symptom (broken button) as the problem, when the real problem (why users stopped sharing) remains unsolved. Fixing the bug will get some metrics moving, but you'll leave the root cause untouched—so sharing will still be suppressed by whatever changed seven weeks ago. You ship, celebrate a fix, and watch shares plateau far below where they should be.
options.explanation: This captures the core frame reasoning: distinguishing symptom from root cause. The bug is real and must be fixed, but it's a *consequence* of a deeper shift in user behavior or product behavior that started earlier. Shipping the fix without understanding why users lost interest in sharing means you're addressing the wrong problem—the wrong level of causation. The answer shows that the 40% drop is the *actual problem to solve*, not the broken button.

options.option_text: You'll fix the technical debt but miss insight into user behavior. The button will work again, but if users aren't interested in sharing because of a feature change, UI friction, or algorithmic change from seven weeks ago, those blockers stay in place. You'll have solved engineering but not product.
options.explanation: This answer correctly identifies that there's a product-level cause separate from the engineering bug, and hints at the right mental model (feature/UX/algo changes as root causes). It shows empathy for the real user friction. However, it's framed defensively—as 'missing insight'—rather than directly naming the risk: *you won't restore the behavior you're trying to optimize for*. It's good reasoning but less precise about the consequence.

options.option_text: You might ship a buggy fix that doesn't fully work, so you'll have to patch it again later. Also, users might distrust the feature after it failed, so adoption will be slow even after the fix.
options.explanation: This focuses only on the visible symptom layer: the button doesn't work, users lose trust, QA risk. These are real concerns but they're about the bug itself, not the frame question. The answer doesn't grapple with the seven-week drop or acknowledge that something *else* suppressed sharing before the button broke. It treats the question as 'will the fix work?' instead of 'are we solving the right problem?'

options.option_text: You risk slowing down the fix unnecessarily. The seven-week drop is water under the bridge—users have moved on. Ship the fix immediately to start rebuilding trust and momentum. Chasing root causes is analysis paralysis; action beats perfection.
options.explanation: This sounds action-oriented and confident, which is appealing. But it commits the core error the scenario is teaching: it assumes that fixing the button is sufficient to restore the behavior Priya is trying to move. If the seven-week drop was driven by something else—a UI change, competitive move, algorithm shift—then sharing will *stay* suppressed even after the button works. This conflates 'ship faster' with 'ship smarter.' It optimizes for closure, not for understanding the actual problem.

list.step_nudge: Map the full space. There are multiple things that could cause story shares to crater—not just the button.

questions.question_text: List all the things that happened between week 1 (the drop) and week 3 (the bug appeared). What data points does Priya have access to?

options.option_text: Priya should map the 40% drop timeline against product events (new features, UI changes, algorithm shifts), user behavior shifts (engagement patterns, feature usage), infrastructure changes (API latency, notification delivery), and competitive context (new platforms, features). She should cross-reference server logs, analytics dashboards (feature funnels, session flows), user feedback (support tickets, reviews), and retention cohorts to identify what changed at week 1 that made story sharing less appealing before the bug materialized.
options.explanation: This identifies the core reasoning move: expanding the stakeholder/system map to see the causal system, not just the symptom. It distinguishes between internal product changes and external factors, recognizes that bugs don't cause drops—only reduced appeal does. It names specific data sources (logs, funnels, cohorts, feedback) and the temporal logic: the drop happened before the bug, so the root cause is something that made the feature less attractive, not a technical failure. This is frame→list work: scoping the real problem before fixing the visible one.

options.option_text: Priya should check what product changes shipped in week 1: new UI, algorithm changes, feature deprecations, or permission changes. She should also look at server metrics like latency or error rates. Once she knows what changed, she can see if the drop correlates with the product change, then ship her bug fix and monitor if the drop reverses.
options.explanation: This captures the right mental model—look for what changed at week 1, and focus on product/infrastructure changes. But it's missing two critical elements: (1) it doesn't expand to user-facing context (competitive moves, user sentiment, feature adoption shifts) beyond internal product events, and (2) it skips the step of distinguishing between the root cause and correlation. It assumes the drop is tied to a product change without considering that something external or behavioral might have changed first. It also doesn't name specific data sources (funnels, cohorts, feedback) beyond generic 'metrics.''

options.option_text: Priya should ship the bug fix immediately since it's been tested and ready. After the fix is live, she can monitor share metrics to see if they recover. If they don't, then she can investigate other causes. The most important thing is to unblock the button and clear the tech debt.
options.explanation: This addresses the symptom (the broken button) but ignores the question entirely. The question is about discovering the root cause of the 40% drop, not about shipping the bug fix. This conflates two problems: the technical bug (a blocker to fix) and the product problem (why users stopped sharing). It optimizes for engineering velocity (clear the ticket) rather than understanding the real problem. It's a junior answer because it assumes shipping a fix to a bug that existed for 3 weeks will suddenly reverse a 7-week trend—backward reasoning that ignores causality.

options.option_text: The 40% drop and the bug are likely the same root cause—a systemic backend issue introduced 7 weeks ago that started with silent failures, then materialized as the visible race condition in week 3. Priya should investigate the infrastructure/deployment history from 7 weeks ago and look for a single change that cascaded into both the drop and the bug.
options.explanation: This sounds technically reasonable (a single root cause) but contradicts the timeline evidence. The bug existed for only 3 weeks; the drop existed for 7. If they were the same root cause, the drop should have started when the bug appeared, not 4 weeks earlier. This answer mistakes correlation for causation and assumes a linear infrastructure failure explains both phenomena. It also narrows the solution space to backend/infrastructure when the real cause could be a product decision, user behavior shift, or competitive pressure—all invisible to logs. This is plausible-wrong because it optimizes for technical elegance over evidence.

questions.question_text: Besides 'button broken,' what are three other reasons someone might stop sharing stories to their followers?

options.option_text: The feed algorithm changed to deprioritize story posts, reducing visibility of shared content. Users saw fewer engagement signals (likes, comments) on their stories, so the behavior felt less rewarding and they stopped trying.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the core 'list' reasoning move: expanding the stakeholder map to include algorithmic incentives, not just user intent. It identifies a second-order effect (reduced feedback loop → reduced motivation) that would cause a behavior crater seven weeks before the button broke. It's specific, testable, and shows why the symptom (button failure) is separate from the root cause (depleted motivation). A PM or data analyst would investigate feed ranking changes in week 0.

options.option_text: Lens introduced a new 'story expiration' feature that deletes stories after 24 hours. Users found this limiting and saw less point in sharing ephemeral content compared to permanent posts, so adoption dropped.
options.explanation: Right structural direction—identifies a product change that shifts the value proposition of stories. Shows empathy for user motivation. But it's less complete than A because it assumes users would consciously decide 'this feature makes stories less valuable,' when the real impact is often more subtle: reduced engagement, network effects, or unexpected friction. Still, it's a legitimate hypothesis a junior PM might explore in the stakeholder map.

options.option_text: Users found stories too difficult to create. The UI was confusing, or there were too many steps to post a story to their followers.
options.explanation: This stays at the visible symptom level: 'the feature is hard to use.' While UI friction could contribute to a drop, it doesn't explain *why* the drop happened *seven weeks ago* in a way that's distinct from the button bug. It's reactive (fix the UI) rather than diagnostic (what changed in the product or market?). A junior engineer might suggest this, but it misses the 'list' frame: you're not looking for 'button broken' analogues—you're expanding the map of *why people care about the behavior at all*.

options.option_text: Competitors like Instagram and TikTok launched better story features, so Lens users migrated to those platforms. Lens needs to match their features to win back users.
options.explanation: Sounds reasonable (market competition is real), but it's a trap. First, if users are fleeing to competitors entirely, we'd see drops *across all behaviors*, not a targeted 40% drop in one feature. Second, this reasoning skips the diagnostic work—it jumps to 'we need to build more features to compete' before even understanding what broke *internally*. It optimizes for the wrong thing (feature parity) and ignores the possibility that something in Lens's own product or incentives changed. Priya should diagnose first, then decide on strategy.

questions.question_text: Who would Priya need to talk to—beyond logs and metrics—to understand the real shift?

options.option_text: [Option A — needs content]
options.explanation: Demonstrates the correct reasoning move for this step.

options.option_text: [Option B — needs content]
options.explanation: Right direction but incomplete.

options.option_text: [Option C — needs content]
options.explanation: Addresses surface symptom only.

options.option_text: [Option D — needs content]
options.explanation: Sounds reasonable but is wrong.

optimize.step_nudge: Compare your options against what matters. What are you optimizing for—a quick win, or user behavior?

questions.question_text: If Priya ships the fix now, what does she learn in the next two weeks? If she digs into the drop first, what does she learn?

options.option_text: Ship the fix now: she learns whether the silent bug was responsible for part of the 40% drop and gains two weeks of user signal on whether fixing the button mechanics alone recovers engagement. Dig first: she learns what changed seven weeks ago that made story sharing less appealing (feature removal? algorithm change? UX friction?), and can test whether the appeal problem exists independently of the button working, which tells her if the fix alone will move the needle or if she needs a second fix.
options.explanation: This answer articulates the core reasoning move for the optimize step: naming the specific information each path yields, and connecting that to the decision criterion (whether fixing the button is sufficient). It shows that the real choice is between optimizing for 'fast closure' vs. 'complete root cause,' and that the right answer depends on understanding how many broken things are in play. The answer avoids the false binary of 'fix vs. investigate'—it shows what each teaches and why that matters to the decision.

options.option_text: Ship the fix now: she can measure if the silent bug was the primary cause of the drop by seeing if engagement recovers. Dig first: she can identify what reduced story appeal and prioritize the bigger problem. The trade-off is speed to value vs. getting the root cause right.
options.explanation: This answer correctly identifies the two learning paths and the speed-vs-completeness trade-off, but it stops short of articulating the real sacrifice: it doesn't name what happens if she ships the fix and it doesn't move the needle (two weeks lost, and then she still has to investigate). It identifies the frame but doesn't complete the optimize step by showing the second-order consequence or the specific metric that should drive the choice.

options.option_text: Ship the fix now: she learns if the button bug was the reason for the drop. Dig first: she learns what else broke. It's better to dig first because bugs always hide bigger problems.
options.explanation: This answer addresses the visible symptom (two bugs exist) without the reasoning that matters. It offers no sacrifice, no metric, and the closing statement 'bugs always hide bigger problems' is a heuristic, not a defensible choice. It misses the optimize move entirely—no criterion, no guard rail, no temporal analysis of what two weeks of data actually teaches.

options.option_text: Ship the fix now: she learns the fix works and closes the ticket, which is efficient. Dig first: she might not find anything, which wastes time. The right move is ship the fix because speed to shipping is always the optimization criterion in a sprint.
options.explanation: This answer sounds reasonable on the surface (speed matters, shipping is good), but it optimizes for the wrong criterion entirely. It treats 'sprint velocity' as the goal rather than 'understanding what broke and fixing it comprehensively.' It also contains a false premise: 'she might not find anything' ignores that the data already told her something changed seven weeks ago. This answer contradicts the reasoning move of scoping the real problem before committing to a solution.

questions.question_text: The PM is expecting a closed ticket this sprint. What are the consequences of each choice (ship vs. dig) on sprint velocity and on product clarity?

options.option_text: Shipping the fix closes the sprint ticket but trades sprint velocity for product clarity. The silent bug fix is real value—it unblocks the 3% of users hitting the race condition today. But the 40% drop is a separate, earlier problem that will persist and compound. Delaying the fix to investigate costs 3-5 days of sprint time; not investigating costs weeks of unknown product decay. The optimization criterion is: restore the ability to diagnose. Sacrifice: one sprint's velocity. Guardrail: the fix must ship this sprint even if root-cause investigation spills to next sprint—users get the patch, PM gets the ticket closed, and the team gets a bounded investigation window to find what broke story sharing first.
options.explanation: This answer separates the two problems cleanly (local vs. systemic), names the real trade-off (velocity vs. clarity), and proposes an actionable criterion: treat the fix and diagnosis as independent work streams. It acknowledges both constraints (PM's sprint expectation and the unknown 40% drop) and suggests a solution that satisfies both without false either/or reasoning.

options.option_text: Shipping the fix preserves sprint velocity and closes the ticket as promised. The bug fix is a real improvement, even if it only recovers 3% of lost story shares. Root cause for the 40% drop requires product data work that belongs in discovery, not in a bug-fix sprint. The risk of not investigating is that the same unknown problem repeats; the risk of investigating now is that the PM loses confidence in sprint commitments and the team loses momentum.
options.explanation: This recognizes the velocity argument and correctly identifies that investigation might belong in a different work stream. But it stops short of the optimize move: it doesn't name the real sacrifice (what gets delayed if investigation happens?) or propose a specific guardrail (what data do we need, how long, what's the decision threshold?). It frames investigation as purely optional rather than as necessary work that changes the risk picture.

options.option_text: The silent bug fix is worth shipping because it removes friction from the user experience. Users who encounter the failed share will now see their share succeed. This is a pure win for velocity—fix ships, ticket closes, users are happier.
options.explanation: This treats the bug fix as the entire problem and misses the core tension: the 40% drop predates the bug and suggests a structural problem with story sharing appeal. Optimizing only for sprint velocity closure ignores the larger product signal. This is a junior answer that solves the visible ticket without engaging with the 'bigger drop' context that frames the challenge.

options.option_text: Shipping the fix sacrifices product clarity because it rewards the team for solving the symptom rather than the root cause. The PM will be satisfied and the sprint will close, but this encourages a culture of tactical bug fixes over strategic problem-solving. The team should block the fix from shipping until they understand what caused the 40% drop first, even if it means missing the sprint deadline.
options.explanation: This reverses the priority incorrectly. It treats the working fix as a liability ('encourages tactical thinking') and punishes the PM/sprint for wanting closure. The 'block the fix' stance creates a false either/or and ignores that shipping a real improvement while investigating separately is the actual mature move. This optimizes for team virtue-signaling rather than for the user (who is still experiencing the silent bug today).

questions.question_text: What would success look like for each path? Fewer crashes vs. restored user behavior are very different goals.

options.option_text: Success means identifying the root cause of the 7-week drop AND measuring whether fixing it restores story sharing volume back toward baseline, while the silent bug fix prevents future user frustration from the race condition. Two separate metrics: user behavior recovery (primary) and error rate elimination (safety guardrail).
options.explanation: This answer captures the core optimize move: naming distinct success criteria for two different problems, identifying which is the real goal (restoring user behavior, not just crashes), and how they relate (bug fix is necessary but not sufficient). It distinguishes symptom from root cause explicitly and articulates both the optimization criterion (behavior recovery) and the guardrail (error elimination). This shows Priya understands she's solving two problems, not one.

options.option_text: Success is reducing error logs to zero with the bug fix, then investigating what drove the 40% drop separately. Once the button is reliable again, users will naturally share more stories. We measure the fix's success by crash rates, and the drop's success by volume recovery.
options.explanation: This recognizes that there are two separate investigations needed and avoids conflating the bug fix with root cause analysis. However, it assumes that fixing the silent bug alone will restore user behavior—a dangerous assumption given the 7-week gap. It gets the structure of two metrics right but misses the key reasoning: the original drop suggests a behavioral or product change (not a technical one), so the bug fix alone won't reverse it. Missing the causal chain analysis.

options.option_text: Success is shipping the bug fix immediately to eliminate the race condition. Once users can reliably share to stories without errors, the story sharing volume will bounce back. The metric is error rate in the upload queue.
options.explanation: This treats the visible symptom (silent bug) as the only problem and assumes fixing it solves everything. It completely ignores the 7-week drop that predates the bug, which suggests a behavioral or product-level issue unrelated to technical reliability. This is premature closure—shipping only addresses the recent crash, not the deeper decline in user motivation to share. No distinction between crash prevention and behavior recovery.

options.option_text: Success means the story sharing feature works perfectly with zero errors. Ship the bug fix immediately, monitor crash rates daily, and if volume stays down after 2 weeks, escalate to the PM as a separate issue. Technical excellence first, user behavior investigation later if needed.
options.explanation: This sounds reasonable—technical rigor, careful monitoring—but it optimizes for the wrong thing. It treats the bug fix as the primary path forward and treats user behavior recovery as a secondary concern to investigate 'if needed.' This contradicts the optimize move, which is to name the real criterion (restored user behavior) and accept the sacrifice (may require investigating non-technical causes, not just shipping code). The 7-week gap is the real signal; treating it as 'separate' risks shipping a fix that doesn't address why users stopped sharing in the first place.

win.step_nudge: Make a call. You can't do both in one sprint. What do you recommend and why?

questions.question_text: Should Priya ship the button fix now or hold it while she investigates the seven-week drop? State your recommendation as a single sentence.

options.option_text: Ship the fix immediately, but pair it with a two-week diagnostic sprint to identify what caused the original seven-week drop, since fixing the symptom without understanding the root cause means the real problem stays hidden and will likely resurface.
options.explanation: This answer demonstrates the core reasoning move for the 'win' frame: making a specific, defensible, falsifiable call that separates symptoms from root causes. It ships the known fix (unblocks users, removes technical debt) while committing to a bounded investigation of the deeper problem. The two-week timeline is concrete and testable. It rejects the false choice between 'ship now' and 'investigate first' by doing both—acknowledging that the button fix has value independent of the seven-week mystery, but that mystery still requires investigation to prevent recurrence.

options.option_text: Hold the button fix and investigate the seven-week drop first, because you can't ship any fix until you understand the full scope of what broke.
options.explanation: This answer correctly prioritizes root-cause investigation and rejects shipping a patch without context. It shows good instinct about the bigger problem. However, it misses a key optimization: the button fix is a known, solvable problem that provides immediate user value while investigation happens in parallel. It creates a false dependency—treating the bug fix as blocked on the investigation, when in reality they are orthogonal concerns that can both move forward. A complete answer would decouple them.

options.option_text: Ship the button fix now because it's a clear bug that's been in production for three weeks and users are actively being frustrated by the silent failure.
options.explanation: This answer focuses on the visible, tactical symptom—the race condition Priya found—and optimizes for closing the ticket quickly. It is locally correct (the bug is real and causes real friction) but completely ignores the bigger signal: a 40% drop that predates this bug by four weeks. Shipping the fix alone means missing the opportunity to diagnose what made story sharing less appealing in the first place, allowing the root problem to continue undermining user engagement even after the button works again.

options.option_text: Hold both the fix and investigation for now, since investing engineering time in either direction is premature until you've surveyed users to understand why story sharing dropped.
options.explanation: This answer sounds cautious and user-centric on the surface, but it actually optimizes for the wrong thing: paralysis. It delays shipping a known fix (which has a clear ROI and removes friction immediately) while waiting for research that may or may not change the engineering direction. It confuses the *decision framework* (understanding root cause) with the *execution sequence* (you can fix the button while investigating the drop). By delaying the fix, it extends user suffering from a broken feature for an unknown amount of time, buying little new information that would change the button-fix decision itself.

questions.question_text: What is the one piece of evidence that would tell Priya whether her recommendation was right?

options.option_text: After shipping the bug fix, measure whether story shares recover toward their pre-7-week baseline within one week. If they don't recover significantly, the silent bug wasn't the root cause of the drop—something else is still suppressing sharing behavior.
options.explanation: This directly tests the causal hypothesis. The win move requires a falsifiable prediction: if the fix is right, what measurable change should follow, and within what timeframe? This option names the metric (recovery), the threshold (toward baseline), and the timeline (one week). It acknowledges that fixing the silent bug is necessary but may not be sufficient—the real test is whether user behavior actually changes. This is the core reasoning move of 'win': making a specific, testable call that could prove wrong.

options.option_text: Correlate the timing of any product changes made between weeks 1–7 (new UI, algorithm tweaks, feature removals) with the 40% drop to identify what might have suppressed sharing appetite before the silent bug appeared.
options.explanation: This correctly identifies that understanding the root cause requires looking upstream in time—good diagnostic thinking. But it stops at investigation. It doesn't articulate what Priya should *do* with that knowledge, or how she would measure whether her recommended action (ship the fix vs. investigate further vs. do both) was correct. It answers 'what caused the drop?' but not 'was my recommendation right?'

options.option_text: Check whether the silent bug fix resolves the error logs and makes the share button function reliably in QA testing.
options.explanation: This validates that the code fix works technically—the button no longer fails. But it doesn't answer the strategic question at stake: whether shipping this fix alone solves Priya's real problem. The bug fix is necessary but the question is whether it's sufficient. This approach only confirms the symptom is gone, not whether the underlying business drop reverses.

options.option_text: Survey users who stopped sharing stories to find out if they were aware of the share button bug and if that's why they stopped.
options.explanation: This sounds reasonable (ask users!) but it misses the actual evidence structure. The timeline already tells us most users couldn't have experienced the silent bug before the 40% drop began—the bug is 3 weeks old, the drop is 7 weeks old. Users aren't a reliable source for diagnosing a problem that predates their behavior change. This optimizes for directness over rigor and would likely waste time collecting unreliable data.

questions.question_text: If the original drop turns out to be unrelated to the button (like a feature change or a competitor launch), how does that change what Priya does next?

options.option_text: Ship the bug fix immediately to restore the button's reliability, then run a structured experiment: measure story shares before/after the fix to isolate the 40% drop's true cause. If shares don't recover, the original drop is unrelated—pivot to investigating what changed in the app or market seven weeks ago. Use the fix as a control, not a solution.
options.explanation: This embodies the core 'win' move: make a specific, testable call that separates signal from noise. Priya ships the fix (defensible—it's objectively broken), then uses the post-fix data as evidence to either confirm or refute the hypothesis that the button was the root cause. It names the test (measure recovery), the threshold (does it recover to baseline?), and the decision rule (if not, investigate the real cause). This avoids both shipping blind and overweighting a symptom as cause.

options.option_text: Delay shipping the bug fix until the team investigates what caused the original seven-week drop. Once they identify the real driver, ship both fixes together so users see immediate improvement in story shares. This prevents a partial fix from masking the bigger problem.
options.explanation: This correctly prioritizes finding the root cause over symptom-fixing, and recognizes that fixing only the button won't solve the 40% drop. However, it's missing the 'falsifiable call' element. It doesn't say what the threshold is for shipping, or how long the investigation can take, or what evidence would prove the fix is sufficient. It's also risky to delay shipping a known bug—you're gambling that the investigation moves fast enough. A stronger answer would set a timeline and decision criterion.

options.option_text: Ship the bug fix right away. A silent failure is clearly a blocker that frustrates users, and fixing it will help story shares recover toward their previous baseline.
options.explanation: This focuses only on the visible symptom (the broken button) without addressing the core tension: the bug is three weeks old, but the drop is seven weeks old. This answer assumes the fix will solve the problem, but the data contradicts that assumption. It's the 'ship and close the ticket' instinct Priya was warned against—treating a symptom as the root cause just because it's easier to fix.

options.option_text: The original drop and the button bug are clearly two separate issues, so focus engineering effort on preventing similar silent failures across the app rather than investigating the seven-week drop. Shipping the fix sends a signal that quality matters, which will rebuild user trust independent of what caused the initial decline.
options.explanation: This sounds proactive—improve quality broadly, signal trust—but it abandons the core task. It assumes that engineering polish alone will recover a 40% drop, when the PM's data suggests a structural product or market change happened seven weeks ago. It's optimizing for organizational narrative ('we fixed bugs') rather than for user behavior and business impact. Without understanding what broke story sharing in the first place, Priya can't know if shipping a quality fix addresses the real problem or just makes the app work better while the core feature remains unappealing.

title: API Docs at Stripe: Time vs. Trust
scenario_context: Marcus is a staff engineer on Stripe's developer experience team. For six months, his team has manually written API reference docs by parsing code comments, crafting examples, and ensuring consistency across hundreds of endpoints. The process takes three weeks per release. The PM has introduced an AI writing tool that can auto-generate docs from code comments in just a few hours. Marcus knows from experience that developer NPS correlates tightly with documentation quality, and the AI tool excels at breadth but struggles with depth—it catches the happy path but misses edge cases, warnings, and nuanced details that senior engineers rely on.
scenario_trigger: The PM needs a decision by Friday on whether to adopt the AI tool. The tool would likely catch about 80% of what human writers catch, freeing up 18 weeks per year across six releases.
scenario_question: What trade-off is Marcus actually making, and how should he present it to the PM?
engineer_standout: Think about who reads these docs and when they matter most. Is quality equally important for every audience and every use case?

frame.step_nudge: Define what you're actually choosing between. It's not time vs. effort—it's time vs. a specific kind of trust.

questions.question_text: What is the relationship between doc quality and Stripe's developer retention or NPS? What evidence does Marcus have?

options.option_text: Doc quality directly affects developer trust and retention because developers choose APIs based on confidence they can successfully integrate. Poor docs force developers to guess, file issues, or switch to competitors. Stripe's historical NPS data likely shows that developers who report 'clear, thorough API documentation' as a strength stay longer and expand usage. The 20% gap the AI tool misses—edge cases, warnings, 'what Junior engineers get wrong'—directly correlates with the support tickets and churn that hurt retention metrics.
options.explanation: This answer identifies the causal mechanism (developer confidence → integration success → retention) and anchors it to measurable evidence Marcus likely has (NPS correlation with doc quality, support ticket volume). It names the specific risk from the 20% gap, showing Marcus understands the trade-off is not just 'speed vs. quality in the abstract' but 'time saved vs. developer churn.' This is the framing that lets him make a defensible argument to the PM.

options.option_text: Stripe's developer NPS probably correlates with doc quality based on internal surveys or feedback—developers consistently mention documentation as a factor in satisfaction. The missing 20% from AI (edge cases, warnings) matters, but Marcus doesn't have quantified data on exactly how much that 20% drives churn or NPS decline versus other factors like API design or support responsiveness.
options.explanation: This answer correctly identifies that NPS correlation likely exists and that the 20% gap is a real risk. However, it stops at 'probably correlates' and acknowledges lack of quantified impact, which weakens the framing. Marcus needs to articulate *why* the gap matters and *what metrics show it matters*—not just that data might exist somewhere. It's the right direction but leaves the core trade-off vague.

options.option_text: API documentation is important to developer satisfaction, and Stripe's docs are currently good because the team spends three weeks per release on them. Using AI would save time, but developers might notice lower quality if the tool misses details. This could affect satisfaction scores over time.
options.explanation: This answer identifies the surface symptom (docs matter, speed vs. quality trade-off) but doesn't frame the *relationship* or the *evidence*. It doesn't explain *why* doc quality drives NPS, what specific mechanisms are at play, or what data Marcus should reference. It treats the problem as 'docs might get worse' rather than 'this decision determines developer retention and competitive positioning.'

options.option_text: Developer NPS is primarily driven by API performance, pricing, and feature completeness, not documentation quality. While good docs are nice-to-have, most developers who choose Stripe do so for its capabilities and reliability. If the AI tool saves 18 weeks per year, that time can be reinvested in feature work and performance improvements that have a much larger impact on NPS.
options.explanation: This sounds reasonable but contradicts the scenario's premise—Marcus knows 'developer NPS correlates tightly with doc quality.' This answer dismisses that correlation and pivots to a different optimization (features + performance). It's plausible-sounding (feature work does matter) but it misframes the problem and ignores the evidence Marcus already has. It's the argument the PM might make, and exactly what Marcus needs to counter.

questions.question_text: The AI tool is '80% as good' at docs. But 80% of what, exactly? Is it 80% complete coverage or 80% quality on each doc?

options.option_text: It's 80% coverage (the tool documents 80% of endpoints), but unknown quality on each doc. Marcus needs to know: does the 20% gap hit high-traffic endpoints or niche ones? And on the 80% it does cover, are the edge cases and warnings there, or just the happy path? The 'goodness' question is actually two separate questions.
options.explanation: This answer separates coverage from depth—the core insight of the frame step. It recognizes that '80% as good' is ambiguous and that the real risk depends on *which* 20% is missing and *what* quality means on the docs that exist. This is the diagnostic move: clarify what we're actually trading before deciding.

options.option_text: It's probably 80% quality on breadth—the tool captures the main use cases and happy paths across most endpoints. But Marcus should ask: on the 20% of features that are edge cases or error handling, does the quality drop further? That's where developer trust actually breaks.
options.explanation: This answer identifies the right place where quality matters most (edge cases, error handling) and shows intuition that the cost isn't uniform. However, it doesn't fully separate coverage from quality per doc, or dig into *which endpoints* are in that 20% gap. It gets the direction right but stops short of the full diagnostic.

options.option_text: 80% means the AI tool is 80% as good overall. Marcus should compare the time savings (3 weeks vs. a few hours) against the quality loss (20% worse) and decide if the speed is worth it. If developers complain less than expected, it's a win.
options.explanation: This treats '80%' as a single number and optimizes for speed vs. complaints. It misses the frame entirely: the question isn't 'is 80% good enough?' but 'what does 80% actually mean?' and 'what breaks when we lose that 20%?' This is reactive problem-solving, not diagnostic.

options.option_text: It's 80% quality on each individual doc, which means the tool is consistently 20% worse. Marcus should calculate the cost of that gap—maybe it's only 5-10% of developers who hit those edge cases, so the NPS hit is smaller than it looks. The PM was right all along.
options.explanation: This sounds analytical but makes two mistakes: it assumes consistent 20% quality drop across all docs (ignoring that impact varies), and it anchors to the PM's frame (speed wins) rather than Marcus's insight (quality drives NPS). It mistakes 'maybe fewer people care' for 'the trade is good.' This actually argues *away* from Marcus's real concern: that the missing 20% is where trust lives.

questions.question_text: Who benefits from AI-generated docs and who gets hurt? Is it the same across all developer skill levels?

options.option_text: Junior developers benefit most from AI docs (fast, broad coverage), but senior developers and power users get hurt (missing edge cases, warnings, gotchas they depend on). This creates a quality cliff: the tool solves onboarding friction but undermines the developers Stripe relies on to build integrations correctly. The trade isn't 'speed vs. quality'—it's 'onboarding velocity vs. expert reliability.'
options.explanation: This answer correctly identifies that the impact is segmented by skill level, not uniform. It distinguishes *who* benefits (juniors need breadth) from *who* gets hurt (seniors need depth), and frames the actual trade: not a general quality loss, but a specific failure mode that affects the developers most likely to build production systems. This is the 'frame' move—seeing that the symptom (slow docs) masks a root cause (asymmetric impact by developer maturity).

options.option_text: AI docs save time for new developers learning the API, but experienced developers will notice gaps in edge case coverage and warnings. The real question is whether Stripe's audience skews junior (where speed helps adoption) or senior (where trust matters more). Without knowing the developer profile, it's hard to say who wins and loses.
options.explanation: This answer correctly identifies the skill-level split and the difference in what each group values (speed vs. completeness). However, it stops short of the key insight: *Stripe's audience is skewed senior*—the developers building production integrations are more senior, and they're the ones driving NPS. The answer hedges ('without knowing the profile') instead of applying domain expertise to the specific context. It's directionally right but incomplete.

options.option_text: AI-generated docs will help all developers save time since they can get answers faster. Some developers might notice quality issues, but most will be satisfied with the quick turnaround. The speed benefit outweighs minor gaps for most use cases.
options.explanation: This treats 'docs' as a monolithic product and assumes all developers have uniform needs. It misses the critical insight that the 'minor gaps' (edge cases, warnings) are exactly what senior developers rely on to avoid bugs in production. It optimizes for the visible symptom (speed) and dismisses the hidden cost (loss of trust with power users). This is a junior answer that doesn't account for segmented impact.

options.option_text: Both junior and senior developers benefit equally since faster docs mean everyone gets information quicker. If there are quality gaps, Stripe can patch them with feedback loops—developers will flag missing edge cases and Marcus's team can add them later. So there's no real trade: you get speed now, quality improvements later.
options.explanation: This sounds pragmatic but misses the core trade entirely. It assumes quality gaps are fixable post-hoc (feedback loops will catch them), but Marcus's insight is that *developer NPS correlates with doc quality*—meaning the damage (lost trust, wrong integrations, support load) happens before the feedback loop closes it. It also assumes benefits are uniform ('equally'), contradicting the skill-level dynamics. This optimizes for the wrong metric and delays recognizing the real cost.

list.step_nudge: Map the space of outcomes. There are more than two ways to solve the time problem.

questions.question_text: Besides 'use AI for everything' or 'keep writing by hand,' what are two other paths Stripe could take?

options.option_text: Use AI to generate first drafts for all endpoints, then have senior engineers review and add edge cases, warnings, and junior-engineer gotchas. Route only high-risk or frequently-misused endpoints to manual review. Measure: track which AI-generated docs get follow-up questions in support; use that signal to retrain the review threshold.
